description: CLI-first observability platform for backend services. Health checks, endpoint probing, distributed tracing — one command away.

steps: Run the installer — it installs the binary and adds it to PATH automatically
Open a new terminal and run: rukkie login

authentication.note: Credentials are hardcoded for single-user use. Session stored at ~/.rukkie/config.yaml. Sessions last 30 days.

rukkie_yaml.description: Configuration file placed in the root of your project. Required by scan, status, inspect, trace, and watch commands.

rukkie_init_yaml.description: Generated by 'rukkie init' inside a service project

fields.description: Display name shown in CLI output
Jaeger Query UI URL for rukkie trace
Fail if response contains errors key

commands.name: rukkie login
commands.description: Authenticate — required before all other commands
commands.notes: Prompts for password, generates a local JWT, stores session at ~/.rukkie/config.yaml. Sessions last 30 days.

commands.name: rukkie scan
commands.description: Scan all services: health check + endpoint probes. All services scanned concurrently.

flags.description: Show only failing or degraded services

commands.name: rukkie status
commands.description: Alias for rukkie scan — same flags

flags.description: Show only failing or degraded services

commands.name: rukkie inspect
commands.description: Deep-dive into one service: health, dependencies, per-endpoint latency and status codes

commands.name: rukkie trace
commands.description: Fetch and display distributed traces from Jaeger. Requires Jaeger running and observability.jaeger.url set.

flags.description: Fetch a specific trace by ID
Number of recent traces to show

commands.name: rukkie watch
commands.description: Live-updating dashboard that refreshes on an interval. Press Ctrl+C to stop.

commands.name: rukkie init
commands.description: Create rukkie.yaml in the current project directory and print the integration snippet. Auto-detects language from package.json (Node.js), pyproject.toml/requirements.txt (Python), go.mod (Go).

output_example.python:
from rukkie_agent import init_rukkie

init_rukkie(
    service_name="my-project",
    api_key="YOUR_API_KEY",
)

commands.name: rukkie shell
commands.description: Opens the interactive styled terminal (Bubbletea TUI). This is what the Windows installer launches.

shell_navigation.notes: cd and pwd are built-ins that track working directory. All other commands are passed to cmd.exe (Windows) or sh (Unix) with the correct working directory set.

connect_api.description: Dedicated REST API backend for RukkiePulse. All backend services POST here to register and send heartbeats. The CLI GETs here for service status. Deployed to Vercel.

endpoints.description: Register a service and update its heartbeat. Auto-creates the service and API key record on first call — no dashboard setup required.
endpoints.response: { ok: true, service: string, registered?: true }

endpoints.description: Returns all registered services with connection status. Called by rukkie scan / rukkie watch.
endpoints.response: { services: [{ id, name, language, description, activeKeys, lastUsedAt }] }

endpoints.description: Health check. Returns 503 if SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY is not configured.

agents.description: Drop-in SDKs that auto-instrument your service, expose /__rukkie/health, push traces to Jaeger, and connect to the RukkiePulse API on startup.

heartbeat.description: On initRukkie() / init_rukkie(), the agent fires a background POST to https://rukkieapi.vercel.app/v1/connect with the API key as a Bearer token and the serviceName in the body. This auto-registers the service on first call and updates last_used_at on every subsequent call so the terminal and dashboard show connection status.

node.notes: Pass app as the second argument to enable request tracing middleware and auto-register GET /__rukkie/health. connectUrl points to the RukkiePulse Connect API — the service auto-registers on first startup, no dashboard setup needed.

python.usage:
from rukkie_agent import init_rukkie

init_rukkie(
    service_name='auth-service',
    api_key='rk_live_xxx',
)
python.usage_with_app:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from rukkie_agent import init_rukkie

app = FastAPI()

init_rukkie(
    service_name='auth-service',
    api_key='rk_live_xxx',
    app=app,
    dependencies={
        'db': check_db_connection,
        'redis': check_redis_connection,
    }
)

api_keys.description: API keys link backend services to the RukkiePulse dashboard. They authenticate the heartbeat and identify which service is connected.
api_keys.storage: Only the SHA-256 hash is stored in the database. The full key is shown once on creation and never again.

steps: Click + New Service, enter name and language
On the service page click + Generate API Key
Copy the key immediately — it will not be shown again
Add the key to your service via initRukkie({ apiKey: 'rk_live_...' })
To rotate: Revoke the old key, then Generate API Key for a new one

dashboard.description: Web UI to manage services and API keys. Built with Next.js, deployed to Netlify, backed by Supabase.
features: View connection status (Live / Recent / Inactive / Not connected)
Code snippet with ESM/CommonJS toggle for Node.js

jaeger.description: Required for rukkie trace. Runs locally in Docker.

ports.purpose: Jaeger UI + Query API (used by rukkie trace)

edge_functions.name: heartbeat
edge_functions.description: Validates API key by SHA-256 hash, updates last_used_at, returns service name

edge_functions.name: services-status
edge_functions.description: Returns all registered services with their most recent last_used_at. Used by rukkie scan / rukkie watch when no rukkie.yaml is present.

workflows.description: GoReleaser cross-platform build + Inno Setup Windows installer
Build Next.js dashboard and deploy to Netlify via ZIP API

real_world_integration.title: Clockee Server — Full Integration Walkthrough
real_world_integration.description: Step-by-step record of integrating RukkiePulse observability into the Clockee backend (Express.js + PostgreSQL + Socket.io).

steps.title: Install the agent
steps.notes: Already added to dependencies in clockee/backend/server/package.json

steps.title: Register the service in the dashboard
steps.action: Sign in at https://rukkiepulse-dashboard.netlify.app → + New Service → name: 'Clockee Server', language: node → Generate API Key → copy key

steps.title: Bootstrap the agent in app.js
what_this_does: Boots the OpenTelemetry SDK (HTTP + Express auto-instrumentation)
Fires a one-shot heartbeat POST to the Supabase Edge Function
Attaches per-request span middleware to Express
Registers GET /__rukkie/health that checks all listed dependencies

steps.title: Create the observability tracer utility
steps.description: Thin wrappers around the OTel API (already initialised by initRukkie). Provides withSpan() and recordError() helpers for custom spans anywhere in the codebase.
exports: withSpan(spanName, attributes, asyncFn) — wraps an async operation in an OTel span, sets OK/ERROR status automatically
recordError(err, extra) — records an exception on the currently active span; call from catch blocks

steps.title: Wrap the database pool for per-query tracing
steps.description: The pg Pool.query() method is monkey-patched to wrap every promise-style call in an OTel span via withSpan(). Each span carries db.system, db.operation, db.statement, and db.name attributes.
steps.example_span_name: db.query SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE id = $1
steps.notes: Callback-style queries are passed through unchanged to avoid breaking existing code.

steps.title: Record errors on active spans in the global error handler
steps.what_this_does: Attaches the exception and a stack trace to the OTel span that was opened for the failing request, so RukkiePulse (and Jaeger) can surface the error alongside the trace.

steps.title: Add a periodic heartbeat for live dashboard status
steps.description: A startHeartbeat() function is called once the HTTP server is listening. It fires a POST to the heartbeat Edge Function immediately and then every 2 minutes, keeping the dashboard status green without requiring user traffic.

steps.title: Create rukkie.yaml in the service root
steps.content:
service:
  name: Clockee Server
  language: node
  apiKey: rk_live_<your_key>
observability:
  jaeger:
    url: http://localhost:16686
  collector: http://localhost:4317
steps.notes: This file lets 'rukkie scan', 'rukkie watch', and 'rukkie inspect' work when run from the clockee/backend/server/ directory. The CLI reads this file to identify the service.

what_is_traced: Every HTTP request → method, route, status code, duration (via Express auto-instrumentation)
Every PostgreSQL query → operation, sanitised SQL statement, database name (via pool.query wrapper)
Every outgoing HTTP call → Twilio, Termii, Cloudinary, Paystack (via OTel HttpInstrumentation)
Every unhandled error → exception + stack recorded on the active span
WebSocket queries → Socket.io event handlers use the traced pool.query, so DB calls inside them are traced automatically

dashboard_status.live: Service is running and heartbeat was received < 5 minutes ago
dashboard_status.recent: Heartbeat received within the last hour
dashboard_status.never_connected: API key created but no heartbeat ever received

cli_commands_after_integration.notes: Run from any directory — fetches all connected services from cloud if no rukkie.yaml is present
Live-updating dashboard; shows Clockee Server status updating in real-time
Run from clockee/backend/server/ to use the local rukkie.yaml for local health probing

tech_stack.otel: OpenTelemetry (agents push to Jaeger collector on port 4317)
tech_stack.docs: Static HTML + CSS deployed to Netlify
